body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: partial
irrlicht_observes: n/a

applicable: n/a — frozen. The scenario's invariant cannot be exercised by a live codex recording; it is a parser-agnostic tailer-robustness property already covered by synthetic unit tests.

## What the scenario pins

`scenario-meanings.md` defines `streaming-partial-writes` by the `streaming_output` primitive: the assistant message arrives in multiple partial flushes before the turn-end signal, and state must stay `working` across all flushes with no premature `→ ready`. The canonical claudecode recipe (the row template) maps this onto a very specific mechanism: claudecode writes multiple intermediate assistant JSONL lines carrying NON-TERMINAL `stop_reason` values, and `resolveAssistantStreaming` (claudecode parser.go:288) downgrades those to `assistant_streaming`, which `IsAgentDone()` excludes. The `streaming_window` phase asserts no `→ ready` flicker fires across that window until the terminal `end_turn` line lands.

## Why codex can't reproduce the live signal

Codex's rollout transcript model is structurally different. Inspecting a real session (`~/.codex/sessions/2026/05/23/rollout-…019e568a….jsonl`):

- Assistant content is written as `response_item` / `payload.type == "message"` / `role == "assistant"` records whose payload keys are exactly `content, phase, role, type` — there is NO `stop_reason` field, and zero occurrences of `assistant_streaming`, `"stop_reason":"null"`, or `"partial":true` anywhere in the file.
- Each assistant `message` line is appended atomically as one complete JSONL event. There is no non-terminal-stop-reason streaming-chunk family for the parser to downgrade.
- The codex parser (parser.go) has no `assistant_streaming` concept at all. The `working → ready` flip is driven by the discrete `event_msg` / `payload.type == "task_complete"` signal, which parser.go:166-169 maps to `turn_done` (the comment there explicitly notes this exists to AVOID working→ready→working flicker on intermediate assistant messages). So codex has no `resolveAssistantStreaming` invariant to pin.

A true "partial write" — assistant bytes flushed mid-line with no trailing `\n` — is a byte-level filesystem-timing artifact. An interactive driver (`tmux send-keys` + transcript tail) cannot deterministically force codex to leave a JSONL line half-written at the moment the daemon ticks; codex flushes whole append-only lines. Forcing it would be a contrived recording that does not actually test the property.

## Where the real property lives (and is already tested)

Tolerating a partial trailing line is the SHARED tailer's job, not codex-specific. `core/pkg/tailer/tailer.go:414-426` reads with a `bufio.Reader` and, on `errPartialAtEOF`, stops WITHOUT advancing the offset past the partial bytes, so they are re-read next tick once the writer flushes the rest. This is locked down by synthetic unit tests in `core/pkg/tailer/large_line_test.go`: `TestTailAndProcess_PartialTrailingLine` (line 73) writes a complete line followed by partial bytes of an assistant event with no `\n`, asserts the offset does not advance past the partial bytes, then appends the remainder and confirms the line is parsed and the session settles. `TestReadLineCapped_PartialAtEOF` (line 171) covers the same at the reader layer.

Because this invariant is (a) parser-agnostic, (b) a byte-timing artifact a live driver can't deterministically force, and (c) already covered by deterministic synthetic fixtures, a live codex recording adds no fidelity. `irrlicht_observes: n/a` — there is no codex-specific lifecycle event that would prove the partial-write invariant, and the daemon's robustness here is exercised by the tailer unit tests, not by a per-adapter recording.

## Conclusion

Cell frozen at `applicable: n/a`. No recipe, no recording. To unblock would require codex to ship an observable non-terminal streaming-chunk family in its rollout JSONL (none today), or a reframing of the scenario from "live streaming flicker" to "tailer partial-line robustness" — the latter is already green under `go test ./core/pkg/tailer/...`.
caveats: agent_supports is partial, not yes: codex does stream output to the terminal, but the on-disk rollout transcript records whole assistant message lines atomically with no non-terminal stop_reason / streaming-chunk family — so the specific resolveAssistantStreaming invariant this scenario pins has no codex analogue to observe.

The partial-write tolerance the scenario cares about is a shared-tailer property (tailer.go errPartialAtEOF path) covered by synthetic unit tests (large_line_test.go TestTailAndProcess_PartialTrailingLine), not a codex-recordable behavior; a live driver cannot deterministically force a mid-line flush.

sources.note: streaming-partial-writes definition: streaming_output primitive, no premature →ready across partial flushes.
No assistant_streaming / stop_reason handling; turn-end flip driven by task_complete→turn_done (lines 155-170). Comment notes this exists to avoid working→ready→working flicker.
Source is FilesUnderRoot{Dir: sessionsDir(), Parser: JSONLineParser} — append-only rollout JSONL under ~/.codex/sessions.
Lines 414-426: bufio.Reader; on errPartialAtEOF stops without advancing offset past partial bytes, re-read next tick. This is the actual partial-write robustness mechanism, parser-agnostic.
TestTailAndProcess_PartialTrailingLine (line 73) and TestReadLineCapped_PartialAtEOF (line 171): synthetic unit tests that already lock down partial-line tolerance and re-read-once-flushed settling.

Real codex session: assistant message payload keys are content/phase/role/type only (no stop_reason); zero occurrences of assistant_streaming / partial flags; one complete assistant line appended per turn segment, terminated by task_complete.
